examples.description: A generated empty-state view and a severity-mapped notification show how the busy, message, and empty-state feedback contracts map to a host framework while preserving live-region semantics and the enabled action gate.

sections.title: Accessibility Model
sections.purpose: Capture accessibility requirements visible in the public contract.
requirements: Components must support accessible naming and description relationships through public associations or equivalent metadata.
Accessibility roles, popup semantics, and text direction must be declarable as typed public state when applicable.
Keyboard activation and focus behavior are compliance-relevant parts of the interaction contract.
Accessible naming relationships use non-owning associations such as `ariaLabelledBy` and `ariaDescribedBy`, so a control can reference labelling or describing controls without owning them.

The accessible name and accessible description are computed from public metadata first, falling back to associated controls, owned text, and finally a literal `tooltip` or label property when no association is present.
Roles, landmark semantics, and popup semantics such as `ariaHasPopup` must be exposed as typed public state so assistive technology receives the same intent the application declared.
Text direction must be a declarable public property with `LTR`, `RTL`, and `Inherit` values so bidirectional content renders predictably without reading renderer internals.
Action controls must remain operable through keyboard activation and expose a visible, ordered focus target so pointer and keyboard users reach the same public interaction contract.
nonGoals: The accessibility model does not redefine ARIA roles, states, or properties; it maps the public contract onto the established accessibility semantics rather than inventing new ones.
The accessibility model does not standardize private renderer markup, generated DOM attribute order, or platform screen-reader announcement wording.
The accessibility model does not replace the interaction, form, navigation, or feedback rules, but it constrains how those contracts expose names, roles, focus, and direction to assistive technology.

tags.name: accessible-name-association
tags.meaning: `ariaLabelledBy` and `ariaDescribedBy` are non-owning associations that supply the accessible name and description from other controls.

tags.name: semantic-role-state
tags.meaning: Roles and landmark semantics are declared as typed public state rather than inferred from renderer markup.

tags.name: popup-semantics
tags.meaning: Controls that open overlays declare popup semantics such as `ariaHasPopup` so assistive technology can announce the expandable relationship.

tags.name: text-direction
tags.meaning: Text direction is a declarable public property with `LTR`, `RTL`, and `Inherit` values for bidirectional content.

tags.name: keyboard-focus-contract
tags.meaning: Keyboard activation and focus order are part of the public, compliance-relevant interaction contract.

formalDefinitions.definition: The short label exposed to assistive technology, computed from `ariaLabelledBy` associations, owned text, or a literal label property when no association is present.
Supplementary text exposed to assistive technology through `ariaDescribedBy` associations or an equivalent description property.
A non-owning association such as `ariaLabelledBy` that references other controls supplying the accessible name without taking ownership of them.
Typed public state declaring the role, landmark, or popup semantics that assistive technology should report for the control.
A public property declaring `LTR`, `RTL`, or `Inherit` so bidirectional text renders deterministically from the public contract.

usage: Provide an accessible name through an `ariaLabelledBy` association when a visible label control exists, and reserve literal label or `tooltip` properties for controls that have no on-screen labelling control.
Attach supplementary help, hint, or error text through `ariaDescribedBy` associations so the description stays linked to the control even when the text moves in the layout.
Declare roles, landmark semantics, and popup semantics such as `ariaHasPopup` as typed public state so generators emit the correct ARIA attributes without inspecting renderer internals.
Set `textDirection` to `Inherit` by default and only override it to `RTL` or `LTR` for content whose direction differs from the surrounding context.

Keep keyboard activation equivalent to pointer activation for action controls, preserve a logical focus order, and ensure disabled controls drop out of the focus order in line with the interaction model enabled gate.
implementationNotes: Extractors should normalize accessibility associations and typed role, popup, and direction state from control metadata instead of scraping rendered ARIA attributes.

Generators should map labelling and describing associations to `aria-labelledby` and `aria-describedby`, and typed role, popup, and direction state to their corresponding ARIA attributes while preserving the enabled focus gate.
Conformance tests should cover accessible name and description computation, role and popup semantics, text-direction handling, and keyboard activation equivalence for action controls.

examples.title: Accessible name and description associations example
examples.description: An input references a label control through `ariaLabelledBy` and a hint control through `ariaDescribedBy`, computing its accessible name and description from non-owning associations.

examples.title: Role and popup semantics example
examples.description: A button declares its role and `ariaHasPopup` semantics as typed public state so assistive technology announces that activation opens a menu.

examples.title: Text direction property example
examples.description: A text control declares `textDirection` of `RTL` so bidirectional content renders deterministically, while the default `Inherit` follows the surrounding context.

examples.title: Generator accessibility binding example
examples.description: A generator maps labelling and describing associations to `aria-labelledby` and `aria-describedby`, preserving the accessible name and description from the public contract.

sections.title: Theming / Design Tokens
sections.purpose: Describe visual customization requirements.
requirements: Libraries must support theme-specific assets and shared theme-level styling resources.
Theme composition must allow base styles plus theme overrides without changing the public component contract.
Density-aware and theme-aware assets are part of the visual system and must remain externally configurable.
Visual values must be expressed as named design tokens so themes can re-skin components without editing component markup or logic.
Themes must be swappable at the application or root scope at runtime, and switching a theme must not require recompiling or changing any component's public contract.
Density must be selected externally through a root-level mode such as cozy or compact rather than encoded as a per-component property.
Design tokens must resolve to a defined fallback when a theme does not provide a value, so components render predictably under any theme.
Theme-specific assets such as images and fonts must be referenced through theme-resolved paths rather than hard-coded URLs so the correct asset loads per theme.
nonGoals: The theming model does not define a specific color palette, typography scale, or the visual design of any individual theme; it standardizes how tokens and overrides are structured, not their values.
The theming model does not standardize private CSS class names, generated stylesheet ordering, or renderer-internal markup used to apply a theme.
The theming model does not replace the layout, component, or accessibility rules; it constrains how visual values are tokenized and themed without changing those public contracts.

tags.name: theme-asset-set
tags.meaning: Libraries ship theme-specific assets and shared theme-level styling resources that vary by theme without touching the component contract.

tags.name: theme-composition
tags.meaning: A theme is composed of base styles plus theme overrides, so switching themes never changes a component's public API.

tags.name: design-token
tags.meaning: Visual values are expressed as named design tokens, such as theming parameters or CSS custom properties, rather than hard-coded literals.

tags.name: density-aware-styling
tags.meaning: Density such as cozy or compact is an externally configurable visual mode applied at a root scope, not a per-component property.

tags.name: external-theme-configuration
tags.meaning: Theme and density selection are configured externally at the application or root level and remain swappable at runtime.

formalDefinitions.definition: A named collection of design-token values and assets that determines the visual appearance of all components without altering their public contract.
A named, themeable styling value such as a color, spacing, typography, or elevation that is resolved at runtime so components reference the token instead of a literal value.
The theme-independent structural styles a component always carries, over which theme overrides are layered.
Theme-specific token values or assets layered on top of base styles to produce a particular theme's appearance.
An externally configurable visual mode such as cozy or compact applied at a root scope to adjust spacing and sizing across components.

usage: Reference colors, spacing, typography, and elevation through design tokens, such as the Material system CSS custom properties `--mat-sys-primary` and `--mat-sys-on-primary`, instead of literal values so a new theme re-skins the component automatically.
Keep theme-independent structure in base styles and layer only token values and assets as theme overrides, so the public component contract never changes between themes.
Apply density at a root scope, for example a `cozy` or `compact` body class, rather than adding density properties to individual components.
Provide a defined fallback for every design token so components stay legible when a theme omits a value.
Resolve theme-specific assets through theme-aware paths or tokens so switching themes loads the matching image and font set without code changes.
implementationNotes: Extractors should record design-token references, theme override layers, and density modes from library styling metadata instead of capturing computed pixel values from a single rendered theme.
Generators should emit token references such as CSS custom properties and density-scoped classes so the output re-skins with the active theme and density without per-component edits.
Conformance tests should cover token-driven styling, base-plus-override composition, runtime theme swapping, and density mode application.

examples.title: Design tokens instead of literal values example
examples.description: A button references named design tokens for background, color, and border radius so switching the theme re-skins it without changing the component contract.

examples.title: Base styles plus theme override example
examples.description: A panel keeps theme-independent base styles while each theme layers its own token values, leaving the public contract unchanged across light and dark themes.

examples.title: Density mode example
examples.description: Density is selected once at the root scope, such as `sapUiSizeCompact`, so descendant components adapt spacing through density-aware tokens instead of per-component properties.

examples.title: Generator design-token binding example
examples.description: A generator emits Material system CSS custom properties and a density-scoped class so the active theme and density re-skin the button without editing the component.

sections.title: Internationalization
sections.purpose: Describe locale and translation requirements.
requirements: Libraries must provide translatable message resources for public UI text.
Locale-aware rendering must support right-to-left and left-to-right presentation where applicable.
Public text-bearing components must expose text direction and translatable labels through explicit metadata.
Translatable public text must be referenced through stable message keys resolved from locale-specific message resource bundles rather than hard-coded literals.
Message resource bundles must resolve through a deterministic locale fallback chain from the most specific locale to a default bundle.
Locale-sensitive values such as dates, numbers, and currencies must render through typed, locale-aware formatting rather than fixed presentation.
The active locale and text direction must be externally configurable application-level settings that components inherit rather than hard-code.
Translatable properties must be marked in public metadata so tooling can extract message keys and reconstruct localized output without reading renderer internals.
nonGoals: The internationalization model does not mandate a specific message-bundle file format, translation workflow, or translation management system.
The internationalization model does not define how translated strings are authored, reviewed, or stored; it only constrains how they are referenced and resolved through the public contract.
The internationalization model does not replace the accessibility, theming, or layout rules, but it constrains how those contracts expose translatable text, locale, and direction.

tags.name: translatable-text
tags.meaning: Public text-bearing properties reference translatable message keys instead of hard-coded literals.

tags.name: message-resource-bundle
tags.meaning: Locale-specific message bundles supply translated text resolved by message key.

tags.name: locale-fallback
tags.meaning: Message resolution follows a deterministic fallback chain from the most specific locale to a default bundle.

tags.name: locale-aware-formatting
tags.meaning: Typed values such as dates, numbers, and currencies render according to the active locale.

tags.name: text-direction
tags.meaning: Text direction is a declarable public property with `LTR`, `RTL`, and `Inherit` values for bidirectional content.

tags.name: locale-configuration
tags.meaning: The active locale and direction are externally configurable application settings that components inherit.

formalDefinitions.definition: A locale-specific set of key-to-text entries that supplies translated public text resolved by message key.
A stable identifier that references a translatable string in a message resource bundle, decoupling the public text from any single literal.
The language and regional identifier (such as `en`, `de`, or `ar-EG`) that selects message bundles and locale-aware formatting.
The deterministic resolution order from the most specific locale to a default bundle, used when a key is missing for the active locale.
A property marked in public metadata as carrying user-facing text, signalling that its value is a message key resolved from a bundle.
A public property declaring `LTR`, `RTL`, or `Inherit` so bidirectional text renders deterministically from the public contract.

usage: Reference user-facing text through message keys resolved from message resource bundles, and reserve literal text for non-translatable technical identifiers.
Mark text-bearing properties as translatable in public metadata so extractors can collect message keys without scraping rendered output.
Ship one message bundle per supported locale and rely on the locale fallback chain so a missing translation degrades to a less specific bundle rather than failing.
Render dates, numbers, and currencies through typed locale-aware formatting bound to the active locale instead of formatting values with fixed patterns.
Inherit the active locale and `textDirection` from application-level configuration, overriding direction only for content whose direction differs from its surrounding context.
implementationNotes: Extractors should normalize translatable property flags, message keys, and typed locale-aware value types from component metadata instead of reading literal rendered text.
Generators should map message keys to the target framework's i18n mechanism and typed locale-aware values to locale-bound formatting, while binding text direction to the active locale.
Conformance tests should cover message-key resolution, locale fallback, locale-aware formatting, and text-direction inheritance for text-bearing components.

examples.title: Translatable property and message bundle example
examples.description: A button's `text` property is marked translatable and carries a message key that each locale bundle resolves to localized text instead of a hard-coded literal.

examples.title: Locale fallback resolution example
examples.description: With active locale `de-AT`, resolution walks the fallback chain so a key resolves from the `de` bundle while a missing key degrades to the `en` default rather than failing.

examples.title: Locale-aware value formatting example
examples.description: A numeric value renders through typed locale-aware formatting so the same `1234.5` becomes `1.234,5` under `de-DE`, following the active locale instead of a fixed pattern.

examples.title: Generator internationalization binding example
examples.description: A generator maps the translatable `text` property to a resolved message accessor and binds text direction to the active locale, preserving the localized text and direction from the public contract.

sections.title: Security / Privacy UI Rules
sections.purpose: State UI-facing security and privacy constraints.
requirements: Public component contracts must not rely on unsafe renderer side effects for user-visible semantics.
Components that open or reference popups must declare that relationship explicitly through public metadata.
Implementations should encode or sanitize user-provided visual values before they affect rendering or styling.

sections.title: Performance Requirements
sections.purpose: Describe performance-oriented expectations that follow from the public model.
requirements: Public library catalogs should allow eager discovery of available components without eager loading every implementation detail.
Machine-readable API projections should be derivable from the same public contract used by runtime metadata.
Visual assets and behavior should support lazy or conditional loading when the public contract permits it.

sections.title: Extension Model
sections.purpose: Describe supported extension points.
requirements: The framework must allow new controls, elements, types, and interfaces to be introduced through the same catalog and metadata model.
Design-time metadata may extend authoring semantics without redefining the runtime component contract.
Extension points must preserve compatibility with theming, accessibility, and interaction rules.
Extension artifacts must publish a stable identity, owning library, version, dependencies, and metadata so tooling can discover them without reading implementation code.
Runtime extensions must use public metadata members such as properties, aggregations, associations, events, interfaces, and types rather than private renderer state or DOM structure.
Extension points must declare their allowed content type, multiplicity, lifecycle ownership, and compatibility constraints before applications inject or replace content.
Design-time extensions must be optional metadata overlays that describe palette placement, authoring labels, allowed drops, and edit actions while preserving the runtime contract.

Renderer and drag-and-drop extensions must remain declarative: renderer modules, drop targets, drag sources, and drop effects are named in metadata and must not require consumers to patch internal control code.
Extension contracts must be versioned and additive by default; incompatible changes require a new artifact identity, major version, or explicitly declared compatibility gate.
nonGoals: The extension model does not define a plug-in package manager, transport format, marketplace, or deployment mechanism.
The extension model does not allow applications to depend on private renderer DOM, private state, or undocumented lifecycle hooks.
The extension model does not prescribe a specific design-time tool, IDE, visual editor, or palette layout.
The extension model does not override the component, theming, accessibility, interaction, security, or performance sections; extensions must satisfy those contracts.

tags.name: extension-artifact
tags.description: A catalog-published control, element, type, interface, renderer, or design-time overlay that can be discovered and versioned independently.

tags.name: extension-point-contract
tags.description: A named public slot or capability that declares allowed content type, multiplicity, ownership, and compatibility constraints.

tags.name: metadata-extension
tags.description: An additive metadata contribution that introduces properties, aggregations, associations, events, interfaces, or types through the standard component model.

tags.name: design-time-extension
tags.description: Optional authoring metadata for labels, palette placement, edit actions, and drag-and-drop hints that does not change runtime behavior.

tags.name: renderer-extension
tags.description: A declarative renderer linkage or renderer hook that remains compatible with theming, accessibility, and performance rules.

tags.name: drag-drop-extension
tags.description: A declarative drag source or drop target capability associated with public aggregations and typed drop effects.

tags.name: compatibility-gate
tags.description: Version, feature, or capability metadata that prevents incompatible extension contracts from being consumed silently.

formalDefinitions.definition: A catalog-addressable item published by a library to extend the framework, such as a control, element, type, interface, renderer, or design-time overlay.
A named public location or capability where extension artifacts may contribute content or behavior under declared type, multiplicity, and lifecycle constraints.
An additive contribution to the public metadata contract using the same property, aggregation, association, event, type, and interface structures as built-in components.
Metadata consumed by authoring tools to describe labels, palette grouping, editable properties, allowed drops, and contextual actions without changing runtime APIs.
Declarative renderer linkage that controls how an artifact is rendered while preserving public theming, accessibility, and interaction contracts.
Metadata that declares which public aggregation can act as a drag source or drop target and which drop effects are supported.
A version, feature flag, required capability, or peer dependency that determines whether a consumer can safely load an extension artifact.

usage: Publish extensions through the same library catalog as built-in artifacts so generators, validators, and documentation tools resolve them from one source of truth.

Prefer explicit extension-point aggregations or interfaces over ad-hoc callbacks; consumers should know the accepted child type, multiplicity, ownership, and supported events before injecting content.
Keep runtime metadata and design-time metadata separate: design-time overlays may add authoring labels or drag-and-drop hints, but they must not introduce runtime-only behavior.

Treat renderer linkage as an implementation detail behind a public metadata contract; renderer extensions must keep stable theming tokens, accessible names, keyboard semantics, and performance constraints intact.
Declare drag-and-drop capabilities against public aggregations and include accepted types and drop effects so generators can map them to host-framework primitives safely.
Version extension artifacts additively and use compatibility gates for breaking changes, optional features, or host-framework capability requirements.
Validate extension artifacts with the same compliance tests as built-in controls, including metadata shape, accessibility, theming, interaction, security, and performance checks.
implementationNotes: Generators should resolve extension artifacts from catalog metadata and reject entries whose compatibility gates or required capabilities are not satisfied by the target framework.
Design-time metadata should be loaded as an optional overlay so missing authoring metadata never changes runtime behavior or generated component APIs.
Renderer and drag-and-drop declarations should map only to public host-framework primitives and must not require direct DOM patching or private lifecycle hooks.

examples.title: Extension artifact and public extension point example
examples.description: A catalog-published AnalyticsPanel control contributes to the workspace.cards extension point with a declared interface, multiplicity, and ownership contract.

examples.title: Design-time metadata overlay example
examples.description: A design-time overlay supplies labels, palette grouping, editable properties, and drop hints without changing the runtime component metadata.

examples.title: Renderer and drag-and-drop extension contract example
examples.description: A workspace column links to a renderer module and declares drag source, drop target, drop effects, and compatibility requirements on a public aggregation.

examples.title: Generator extension outlet example
examples.description: A host-framework generator maps an extension point to a typed outlet that accepts only registered extension components satisfying the contract.

sections.title: Compliance Rules
sections.purpose: Summarize what every implementation must satisfy.
requirements: A compliant implementation must publish its public surface through a discoverable library catalog.
A compliant implementation must describe every public component with typed metadata for properties, composition, relationships, and events.
A compliant implementation must preserve accessibility, theming, internationalization, and testing evidence as part of the public contract lifecycle.
Compliance is evaluated against the normalized public contract, not against private source files, renderer internals, generated DOM structure, or framework-specific implementation details.
Every public library, component, element, type, and interface must be reachable from the catalog with a stable identifier, ownership information, version scope, and documented visibility.

Component metadata must include enough typed information for validators and generators to reconstruct properties, aggregations, associations, events, extension hooks, and design-time hints without reverse-engineering runtime behavior.
Cross-cutting rules for accessibility, theming, internationalization, security, performance, and extension compatibility must be traceable to public metadata or documented evidence.
Automated compliance tests must verify that `/openui.json`, generated API projections, documentation, examples, and implementation behavior remain synchronized for every normative public contract.
When an implementation intentionally omits an optional capability, the omission must be explicit and must not weaken required baseline metadata, interaction, accessibility, or evidence rules.
nonGoals: Compliance rules do not prescribe a specific renderer, build system, test runner, package format, or host framework.
Compliance rules do not require implementations to expose private members, internal DOM nodes, renderer helper APIs, or source-code-only conventions.

Compliance rules do not redefine section-specific requirements; they aggregate the mandatory checks from the catalog, component, interaction, state, accessibility, theming, internationalization, and testing sections.
Compliance rules do not certify application-specific business logic, visual design choices, or data schemas beyond their use of the public UI contract.

tags.name: catalog-discoverability
tags.meaning: Every public library member is reachable from a stable, machine-readable catalog entry.

tags.name: metadata-completeness
tags.meaning: Every public component declares typed properties, aggregations, associations, events, and relevant capability metadata.

tags.name: contract-based-validation
tags.meaning: Compliance is evaluated from public metadata, documentation, generated projections, and observable behavior rather than private implementation details.

tags.name: cross-cutting-evidence
tags.meaning: Accessibility, theming, internationalization, security, privacy, performance, and extension requirements are backed by public metadata or documented evidence.

tags.name: testable-conformance
tags.meaning: Every normative rule has an automated or reviewable acceptance check that can be repeated by implementers and validators.

tags.name: explicit-capability-scope
tags.meaning: Optional capabilities are declared as supported or intentionally absent without weakening required baseline conformance.

formalDefinitions.definition: A framework, library, generator, or projection that satisfies the required public contract rules for the specification sections it claims to implement.
The catalog-addressable libraries, controls, elements, types, interfaces, metadata members, events, examples, and generated projections exposed to consumers.
Machine-readable metadata, API projections, documentation, examples, test results, or reviewable records that demonstrate a rule is satisfied.
The declared scope of sections, libraries, components, and optional capabilities that an implementation claims to support.
Any missing, inconsistent, private-only, or untested part of a claimed public contract.
A repeatable test, generated file, documentation page, screenshot, or report used to verify conformance.

usage: Start compliance validation from the catalog root and walk only public entries; any component that cannot be discovered from the catalog is outside the compliant public surface.

Validate metadata completeness before validating behavior so downstream checks can rely on declared property types, aggregation multiplicity, association targets, event parameters, defaults, visibility, and capability flags.
Treat documentation, `/openui.json`, generated `api.json`-style projections, generated examples, and runtime metadata as synchronized views of the same public contract.

Record cross-cutting evidence next to the public contract it supports, such as accessibility associations, theme token usage, localized text handling, popup relationships, or lazy-loading declarations.
Make optional capability support explicit: a component may omit drag-and-drop, design-time metadata, or a generator mapping only when the conformance profile says the capability is not claimed.
Fail compliance when an implementation relies on private renderer details for behavior that the specification requires to be public, even if the visible UI appears correct.
Keep validation artifacts deterministic and versioned so maintainers can compare compliance before and after library, metadata, or generator changes.
implementationNotes: Validators should report compliance gaps with the section, catalog entry, metadata member, evidence artifact, and expected rule so maintainers can correct the public contract without guessing.
Generators should fail fast when required metadata is missing and should preserve optional capability declarations as explicit supported or unsupported features in generated output.
Documentation builds should treat missing examples, stale JSON mappings, or generated projections that disagree with `/openui.json` as compliance failures rather than editorial warnings.

examples.title: Catalog discoverability checklist example
examples.description: A conformance profile lists a catalog with libraries, components, types, and interfaces, then verifies that every claimed public member resolves from the catalog root with a stable identifier and owning library.

examples.title: Metadata completeness gate example
examples.description: A Button contract is compliant when typed text and enabled properties, an ariaLabelledBy association, and the press event are public metadata and the implementation does not require private renderer inspection.

examples.title: Cross-cutting evidence record example
examples.description: An Input contract carries accessibility associations, theme token usage, internationalized text handling, and repeatable tests as evidence tied to the same public component contract.

examples.title: Generator compliance guard example
examples.description: A generator guard verifies that generated inputs mirror properties, outputs mirror events, catalog entries resolve, metadata projections stay synchronized, and accessibility bindings come from public associations.

sections.title: Test & Acceptance Criteria
sections.purpose: Describe how the specification should be verified.
requirements: Every public metadata contract should be verifiable by automated tests that exercise properties, events, and rendering-facing semantics.
Acceptance criteria should cover unit behavior, visual states, accessibility semantics, and responsive or theme-sensitive presentation.
Machine-readable projections such as `api.json` should remain consistent with runtime metadata and documented behavior.

examples.title: UI concept model acceptance test
examples.description: A compliant test should verify that published controls and elements are classified correctly, owned UI regions remain aggregations, semantic references remain associations, and higher-level concepts such as dialogs and forms are represented through metadata-defined composition.

examples.title: Application structure acceptance test
examples.description: A compliant test should verify that every component referenced by the application structure resolves to a declared library dependency, the shell exposes navigation and owned pages through aggregations, non-owning references such as the current page remain associations, and the nested page hierarchy is resolvable from public metadata.

examples.title: Layout system acceptance test
examples.description: Given a normalized specification entry for a Page, Grid, Form, and Board layout contract: verify that named regions such as header, content, and footer are aggregations with declared multiplicity and child types; verify that ordered 0..n regions preserve their declared document order as the normative visual order before responsive adaptation; verify that responsive breakpoints change region arrangement without adding, removing, or reparenting owned content; verify that density and spacing are drawn from a shared, theme-driven scale instead of ad-hoc measurements; and verify that drag-and-drop is present only where a container declares dnd metadata and stays within the target aggregation's child type and multiplicity.

examples.title: Component model acceptance test
examples.description: A compliant test should verify that each component exposes a stable identity, that properties declare types and default values, that aggregations declare child types and multiplicity, that associations remain non-owning references, that events declare typed parameters, and that renderer, design-time, and drag-and-drop metadata stay optional capability hints rather than part of the core contract.

sections.title: Reference Examples
sections.purpose: Provide concrete reference components abstracted from upstream OpenUI5 evidence.
requirements: Reference examples translate concrete upstream component evidence into implementation-neutral specification examples.
A reference action component must preserve its stable identity, owning library, advertised interfaces, public properties, associations, events, and optional capability metadata.

Public properties define the component's configurable state, including text, visual type, width, enabled state, icon configuration, icon ordering, icon density behavior, text direction, accessibility popup state, accessibility role, and badge style.
Hidden properties may be recorded as implementation evidence, but they must not be treated as public API in generated documentation or conformance tests.
Accessibility relationships are represented through non-owning associations such as `ariaLabelledBy` and `ariaDescribedBy`.
User activation is represented by the current public `press` event; deprecated compatibility aliases such as `tap` may be recorded but must not replace the normative activation event.
Optional renderer, design-time, drag-and-drop, badge, and theme-aware visual metadata may be retained as capabilities when they are present in upstream evidence.

Reference examples must include validation expectations that connect the component contract to observable behavior such as state accessors, text direction, activation events, enabled-state gating, visibility, and accessibility metadata.
nonGoals: Reference examples do not standardize OpenUI5 runtime internals, renderer DOM structure, CSS class names, or event ordering.
Reference examples do not require every implementation to expose a button component with the exact same API; they provide concrete evidence for applying the abstract specification models.
Reference examples do not make hidden or deprecated members normative for new generated APIs.
Reference examples do not replace the component, interaction, state, form, accessibility, or theming sections; they demonstrate those sections working together.

tags.name: reference-component-evidence
tags.meaning: A concrete upstream component is captured as evidence for the abstract metadata model.

tags.name: action-component-contract
tags.meaning: A button-like component combines configurable state, accessibility associations, and a public activation event.

tags.name: public-property-surface
tags.meaning: Public properties such as `text`, `type`, `width`, `enabled`, and icon settings define the supported configuration surface.

tags.name: accessibility-association-surface
tags.meaning: Label and description relationships are represented through associations instead of owned content.

tags.name: activation-event-surface
tags.meaning: The current public activation event is normative, while deprecated aliases are compatibility evidence only.

tags.name: optional-capability-evidence
tags.meaning: Renderer, design-time, drag-and-drop, badge, and theme-aware metadata are recorded as optional capabilities.

tags.name: behavioral-validation-evidence
tags.meaning: Reference examples include observable expectations that conformance tests can validate.

formalDefinitions.definition: A concrete, evidence-backed example used to show how the abstract specification models apply to a real component while remaining implementation-neutral.
A button-like component that exposes configurable state, accessibility relationships, and an activation event for application handlers.
Upstream metadata captured for a reference example, including identity, interfaces, properties, associations, events, renderer, design-time, and capability flags.
The set of public properties that applications and generators may use as the component's supported configuration contract.
A deprecated event retained for migration or historical evidence while the current event remains normative.
Metadata that may be preserved when present, such as drag capability, badge styling, renderer linkage, design-time hints, or theme-aware visuals, without becoming mandatory for all components.
An observable behavior or conformance check derived from the reference example, such as enabled-state gating or text-direction preservation.

usage: Use reference examples to verify that the abstract section models can represent a real component without depending on private framework internals.
Preserve identity (`sap.m.Button`), library (`sap.m`), and interface evidence so generated catalogs can link the example back to the component and form/access-key/toolbar contracts it advertises.

Normalize public properties with their declared types, groups, defaults, visibility, and deprecation state; keep hidden properties out of generated public APIs unless a diagnostic view explicitly asks for evidence metadata.
Model `ariaLabelledBy` and `ariaDescribedBy` as multiple non-owning associations so accessibility tooling can resolve labels and descriptions without assuming DOM proximity.
Treat `press` as the normative activation event for new examples and tests; record `tap` only as a deprecated compatibility alias when preserving upstream evidence.
Retain optional renderer, design-time, drag-and-drop, badge, and theme-aware icon metadata as capabilities that enrich documentation and tests without changing the core component contract.

Derive behavioral tests from public behavior: property accessors return normalized values, disabled controls do not dispatch activation, text direction is preserved, visibility follows the public state, and accessibility popup metadata maps to the declared value.
implementationNotes: Extraction pipelines should store reference evidence separately from normative generated APIs so hidden or deprecated members can be audited without being exposed by default.
Generator pipelines should render reference examples using the common component, state, interaction, accessibility, form, and theming models rather than special-casing OpenUI5 runtime behavior.
Conformance tooling should validate the reference example through public metadata and observable behavior, including enabled-state gating for activation and accessibility association preservation.

examples.title: Reference action component metadata example
examples.description: A `sap.m.Button` reference preserves identity, library, interfaces, and public properties such as text, type, width, enabled, icon, text direction, accessibility popup state, accessibility role, and badge style.

examples.title: Accessibility associations and activation event example
examples.description: A reference button exposes `ariaDescribedBy` and `ariaLabelledBy` associations and treats `press` as the normative activation event while retaining deprecated `tap` only as compatibility evidence.

examples.title: Optional capabilities and hidden evidence example
examples.description: Renderer, design-time, drag-and-drop, badge, and theme-aware metadata may be recorded as optional capabilities while hidden properties remain evidence-only.

examples.title: Behavioral validation expectations example
examples.description: Reference validation expectations cover enabled-state activation gating, text-direction preservation, accessibility popup mapping, and public visibility behavior.
